text: Chapter 13 drills chromatic triad/bass studies in Major and Minor Series cycled through the circle of fifths and contrary motion, generated by what Van Eps calls mathematical mechanical motion without regard for the harmonic result (Mechanical motion regardless of harmonic result). The pedagogical mandate is to find and practice nontraditional configurations rather than the well-known ones (Nontraditional configurations must be practiced) and to refuse to flinch from the inevitable clashes—dissonance is a feature of chromatic contrary motion and pedal-bass writing, not a defect to correct (Don't shy from dissonance; Dissonance is natural in contrary motion). The load-bearing analytical premise is that bass and triad are independent voices that incidentally coalesce into four-part chords: the bass operates as an independent fourth voice with its own accidentals, often in a different key from the triad, producing a mixed-accidental enharmonic texture the student must read carefully (Bass as independent fourth voice; Triad and bass as separate identities). Van Eps's preferred generator is the descending circle-of-fifths bass because of its modal neutrality (Cycle of fifths bass is neutral), exploited via the major-makes-minor / minor-makes-major identity that lets a 7th chord be heard as a triad of the opposite modality over a bass (Major makes minor, minor makes major), with inversion arpeggios used to surface which familiar four-note chord is actually present (Inversion arpeggios reveal hidden chords) and the 'chameleon' multi-naming doctrine emphasizing that any voicing has many callings under varied conditions (Chameleon chords). The chapter introduces the bass-suffix chord-symbol notation (e.g., 3B for third-in-bass) without which the Major/Minor Series labels are unreadable (Chord symbol bass-note suffix) and enforces the two-stage discipline rule: strict adherence to the format across all twelve keys first, deviation only after the principle is internalized (Discipline of strict format).
